rulings.Case Title: JAMES KEARNEY VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (BP) AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Withdraw
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, August 31, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 1. Motion to allow the Hoge Fenton firm to withdraw as counsel for defendant The Jack Dymond Company is denied without prejudice to refiling the motion in compliance with the mandatory requirements of CRC 3.1362, including submission of a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1362(e). The Hoge Fenton firm is reminded to submit courtesy copies of all motion papers in the future. =(220/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: GARY HUBBELL VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (BP)AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Albay Construction Co.'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, August 31, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 3. Defendant Albay Construction Co.¿s motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of production, and even if it had, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether plaintiff inhaled respirable asbestos fibers from products and/or materials attributable to defendant. The mere fact that plaintiff wore a paper mask does not require granting defendant¿s motion. Mr. Cohen¿s declaration creates a triable issue on this point. =(220/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES BARAGAR et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (BP) AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, August 31, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 4. Defendant Crane Co.¿s motion for summary judgment and alternate motion for summary adjudication is denied. It appears that defendant satisfied its burden of production by presenting plaintiff¿s factually devoid discovery responses on the issue of whether Mr. Baragar was exposed to any products on the USS Rogers attributable to the defendant. However, when read together and liberally construed in favor of the plaintiffs, 28:23-29:5 and 44:8-45:3 of the deposition of Mr. Pantaleoni, 95:5-13 of Mr. McLean¿s deposition, paragraph 4 of the declaration of Mr. Fening and the declaration of Mr. Baragar create a triable issue on this point. The objections to the cited portions of the deposition testimony of Mr. Pantaleoni and Mr. McLean and to the entirety of the declaration of Mr. Baragar are overruled. The tentative ruling does not rest on nor does the court address either the ¿reentrainment¿ theory or argument about ¿foreseeable use¿ of asbestos-containing products in conjunction with defendant¿s valves. The court rejects plaintiffs¿ position that the so-called ¿Taylor¿ issue of exposure to original parts is an affirmative defense. =(220/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES BARAGAR et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (BP) AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, August 31, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 4. Defendant Gibbs & Cox, Inc.¿s motion for summary judgment is granted. Defendant satisfied its burden of production showing that its sole role with regard to the USS Rogers was the provision of architectural and engineering services and that it did not manufacture, sell or supply any product for that vessel. Plaintiffs failed to create a triable fact on this point. Accordingly, there is no basis for either a strict liability or negligence claim against defendant. (Del Mar Beach Club Owners Assn. v. Imperial Contracting Co. (1981) 123 Cal. App. 3d 898, 914 (strict liability does not lie against engineer and architect); Allied Properties v. John A. Blume & Associates (1972) 25 Cal. App. 3d 848, 855-56 (strict liability does not lie against a marine engineer); Weseloh Family Limited Partnership v. K.L. Wessel Construction Co. (2004) 125 Cal. App. 4th 152, 164-73 (based on extensive discussion of and application of Biankanja and Bily, no duty under negligence law owed by engineer to the owner or general contractor where the engineer provided design services pursuant to a contract with a subcontractor but had no contract with either the building owner or general contractor)). Plaintiffs¿ objections to the declarations of Mr. Sollecito and Admiral Horne as to the character and nature of the work provided by defendant to the Navy (i.e., architectural and engineering services, not manufacture, sale or supply of products) are overruled. Defendant¿s objections to exhibits C, D, E, F, G, H, paragraph 9 of Dr. Richard Cohen¿s declaration, paragraph 4 of Mr. Fening¿s declaration, and the portions of Mr. Kenneth Cohen¿s declaration for this case that relate the character and nature of the work provided by defendant to the Navy are sustained. Plaintiffs¿ request for a continuance of the motion is denied because plaintiffs have failed to show that facts may exist to enable them to oppose the mo
